skill.name: port-allocator
skill.description: Automatically allocate and track development server port ranges for projects that contain package.json, with commands for scanning, listing, and configuration.
skill.summary: Assigns predictable local development port ranges and records them in Claude Code configuration files.
skill.author: guo-yu

security_audit.summary: The skill is documentation-only and does not contain executable code or prompt injection text. However, it instructs users to persist broad Claude Code Bash permissions and read or write hidden files under ~/.claude, including a broad cat pattern, so the static filesystem and command-execution concerns are confirmed while weak-crypto and entropy findings are false positives.

high_findings.title: Broad Persistent Claude Code Bash Permissions
high_findings.description: The skill directs users to add broad allowed Bash patterns to Claude Code settings, including reading all files under ~/.claude with cat and running find, lsof, and dirname patterns. This persists elevated command permissions beyond a single task and may expose sensitive Claude configuration data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission list is explicit and stored in Claude Code settings. The cat ~/.claude/* pattern is broad hidden-directory read access, which is stronger than needed for port allocation.

high_findings.title: Writes to Hidden Claude Configuration Files
high_findings.description: The skill requires reading and writing files under ~/.claude, including settings.json, CLAUDE.md, and port-registry.json. These are global hidden configuration files, so mistakes or malicious follow-on prompts could change agent behavior across projects.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and write instructions are direct evidence. The skill also says to preserve existing content, which reduces destructive risk but not the global configuration risk.

medium_findings.title: Process Termination Commands Require Careful Scope Control
medium_findings.description: The skill documents lsof piped to xargs kill for project ports. It warns against killing unrelated processes, but the commands can still terminate local services if the selected port range is wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The kill commands are real and potentially disruptive, but the surrounding text explicitly restricts their use to current project ports. This makes the concern operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Repository Scan Can Traverse User Code Directories
medium_findings.description: The scan workflow searches a configured code root for package.json files and excludes common build directories. This is legitimate for port discovery, but it still causes broad local filesystem traversal within user-selected source trees.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The find command is specific to package.json and excludes common generated folders. The traversal is expected functionality, but it should remain limited to user-approved code roots.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Obfuscation Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reviewed files are Markdown instructions in English, Japanese, and Chinese. No cryptographic algorithm use, encoded payload, binary content, or obfuscated executable logic was found in the cited skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain front matter and translated prose. High entropy appears caused by non-English text, and the weak-crypto matches do not correspond to real cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command detections are Markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby backtick execution. The command examples still matter for user behavior, but the specific Ruby execution classification is not accurate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is Markdown documentation, and there is no Ruby file or executable Ruby context. The semantic risk comes from the documented shell commands, not Ruby backticks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Hidden Configuration Read Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed command pattern Bash(cat ~/.claude/*) can read any direct file in the hidden Claude configuration directory, not only this skill registry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact broad glob is present in the permission allowlist. It reaches a hidden configuration directory that can contain sensitive agent settings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Port-Based Kill Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented lsof and xargs kill commands terminate processes listening on selected ports. Incorrect port selection can disrupt unrelated local services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command behavior is clear, and xargs kill is potentially disruptive. The risk is moderated by nearby instructions to stay within current project ports.

content.value_statement: Local projects often collide on common development ports, especially when several AI coding sessions run at once. This skill assigns predictable port ranges and records them for Claude Code, Codex, and Claude workflows.
actual_capabilities: Assigns ten-port ranges starting at port 3000 for projects with package.json files.
Lists existing project-to-port assignments from a local registry.
Scans a configured code root for package.json files while skipping common build folders.
Sets or auto-detects a primary code directory for future scans.
Documents Claude Code permission setup for the commands it expects to use.
limitations: Requires local filesystem access to Claude configuration files under ~/.claude.
Targets JavaScript-style projects that contain package.json files.
Does not verify that assigned ports are currently free before all future use.
Permission setup is broad and should be reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Coordinate parallel app sessions
use_cases.description: Assign separate port ranges when several local web apps are open at the same time.

use_cases.title: Standardize team port conventions
use_cases.description: Create repeatable port ranges for project repositories so local services use predictable addresses.

use_cases.title: Audit local development ports
use_cases.description: List registered projects and confirm which ports each one should use before starting services.

prompt_templates.title: Check this project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for the current repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use port-allocator to show the assigned port range for my current project.

prompt_templates.title: List all assignments
prompt_templates.prompt: Use port-allocator list and summarize all registered projects with their port ranges.

prompt_templates.title: Configure code root
prompt_templates.scenario: Set a known source directory before scanning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure port-allocator to use my main code directory, then confirm the saved path.

prompt_templates.title: Scan and report gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan my configured code root, register new package.json projects, and report newly allocated ranges separately from existing ones.

output_examples.input: Show the port range for the current package.json project.
output_examples.output: The project is registered with ports 3000 through 3009. Use 3000 for the main app and 3001 for the API service.

output_examples.input: Scan my code directory for new projects.
output_examples.output: The scan found one new package.json project and assigned ports 3090 through 3099. Build artifact folders were skipped.

best_practices: Review Claude Code permission changes before enabling the allow command.
Keep the configured code root limited to trusted project directories.
Kill only processes inside the assigned range for the current project.
anti_patterns: Do not grant broad ~/.claude file access without checking what files are present.
Do not run process termination commands against ports from another project.
Do not scan your entire home directory when a narrower code root is available.

faq.question: What does this skill allocate?
faq.answer: It allocates ten consecutive local development ports for projects that contain a package.json file.

faq.question: Does it work outside Claude Code?
faq.answer: The instructions are written for Claude Code, but the workflow can inform Claude and Codex sessions.

faq.question: Where does it store assignments?
faq.answer: It documents storage in a port registry file under the hidden ~/.claude directory.

faq.question: Why is the security rating high?
faq.answer: It asks users to persist broad Bash permissions and access hidden Claude configuration files.

faq.question: Can it detect every occupied port?
faq.answer: It documents lsof checks for selected ports, but it is mainly a registry and convention tool.

faq.question: Should I publish it without changes?
faq.answer: No. The permission allowlist should be narrowed before marketplace publication.
